text: During the surgery, the surgeon observes that the afferent limb is positioned dependently, causing food to enter the afferent limb instead of the efferent limb. A partial obstruction is noted at the outflow from the duodenal ileostomy to the efferent limb.
The surgeon uses a ligature device to take down adhesions separating the efferent and afferent limbs, facilitating clearer access between these anatomical structures.

The surgeon places anti-obstruction stitches to reorient the duodenal ileostomy, aiming to facilitate bile flow from the afferent limb to the efferent limb. The stitches are intended to remove the obstruction and allow bile to pass the pylorus smoothly.
The surgeon replaces the anti-obstruction stitches, which have not held, in the duodenal ileostomy area to facilitate bile flow from the afferent limb to the efferent limb.
The surgeon identifies a small piece of silk suture that has pulled out and replaces it with two simple interrupted silk sutures to change the orientation of the afferent limb and the anastomosis.

The surgeon places interrupted sutures to change the orientation of the afferent limb and proceeds to create an enteroenterostomy, 15 centimeters below the duodenal ileostomy. An enterotomy is made on the afferent limb using electrocautery, followed by another enterotomy on the efferent limb, avoiding the anti-mesenteric border to ensure ample space.
The surgeon uses a GIA stapling device to close the enterotomy defect after mating the afferent and efferent limbs together, completing the enteroenterostomy.

The surgeon trims the small bowel while pulling on it to minimize tissue removal. The current configuration shows a wide-open enteroenterostomy. Minimal blood loss of five cc's is noted, and the procedure duration was eighteen minutes.
